title: 2 John
author: 'The elder' — John the apostle
setting: The shortest book in the NT (13 verses). Addressed to 'the elect lady and her children' — likely a local church and its members — warning against welcoming false teachers who deny the incarnation.
purpose: To warn against showing hospitality to teachers who deny that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh — participating in such false teaching by welcoming them — while calling the community to love and to walk in the commandments.
themes: The test of Christology: Jesus came in the flesh
The danger of extending hospitality to false teachers
The commandment to love one another

key_people.name: The Elder
key_people.role: Writes to 'the elect lady and her children' — likely a house church and its members — with a combination of pastoral warmth and doctrinal sternness

key_people.name: The Deceivers
key_people.role: Travelling teachers who deny that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh — the same proto-docetic threat as 1 John, here creating a practical question about hospitality

context: 2 John was written c. AD 90-100, likely from Ephesus, as a follow-up to 1 John addressing a practical problem: should a house church extend hospitality to travelling teachers who deny the Incarnation? The letter's brevity (13 verses) reflects its focused purpose. John's answer is unambiguous: do not receive such teachers into your house or greet them, because to do so is to participate in their evil works.
christ_connection: The doctrinal test of 2 John is identical to 1 John 4:2: those who confess Jesus Christ coming in the flesh are following the teaching of Christ; those who deny it have gone ahead and do not abide in the teaching of Christ (v. 9). Christ himself — specifically the reality of his full human incarnation — is the measure by which all teaching is judged. The letter illustrates the christological principle that heresy is never merely academic: denying the Incarnation has direct practical consequences for how the community uses its common resources and extends its welcome.

key_verses.note: 'This is love, that we walk according to his commandments' — love and obedience are not alternatives; love is expressed through keeping Christ's commandments

'Anyone who does not abide in the teaching of Christ, does not have God... If anyone comes to you and does not bring this teaching, do not receive him into your house' — doctrinal fidelity as the condition of Christian hospitality

themes_detail.title: Truth and Love as Inseparable
themes_detail.text: 2 John holds truth and love together with deliberate care: the greeting ('in truth and love', v. 3), the reason for joy ('your children are walking in the truth', v. 4), and the command ('walk in love', v. 6; 'walk according to his commandments', v. 6) all interweave the two virtues. The letter refuses the false choice between a warm hospitality that ignores doctrine and a doctrinal severity that has no warmth. Genuine Christian love is love 'in truth' (v. 1); it is not divorced from doctrinal discernment but expressed through it.

themes_detail.title: The Limits of Christian Hospitality
themes_detail.text: The letter's most controversial instruction (vv. 10-11) limits hospitality to those who bring the teaching of Christ. In the first-century context, travelling teachers depended on house-church hospitality for their ministry; to receive a teacher was to sponsor his work. The limit John imposes is not social hostility toward heretics but the refusal to fund and platform false teaching. The principle remains: the church's welcome is not unconditional endorsement of every visitor's message, and the community's resources are not to be used to advance teaching that undermines the gospel.

themes_detail.title: Abiding in the Teaching of Christ
themes_detail.text: The phrase 'the teaching of Christ' (v. 9) — the teaching about Christ and the teaching that comes from Christ — is the standard by which travelling teachers are to be evaluated. Those who 'go ahead' of this teaching, transgressing it in the name of spiritual progress or superior knowledge, 'do not have God.' This is John's consistent argument: genuine knowledge of God is inseparable from correct understanding of who Christ is. There is no access to the Father except through the Son (John 14:6), and misrepresenting the Son is not a step toward the Father but away from him.
